name: Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools
districtFact: Largest school district in North Carolina by enrollment, serving Charlotte and all of Mecklenburg County.

about.title: About the District
about.content: Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools (CMS) is the largest school district in North Carolina, serving approximately 147,000 students across 175 schools in Charlotte and Mecklenburg County.

planningTips: Good Friday (Mar 26) is a school holiday in CMS, the Friday before Spring Break starts. This gives students a four-day weekend before the break week — note it on your calendar if you're planning travel.
CMS ends the school year June 9 — later than most families expect. Plan summer camps and childcare through at least the second week of June.
Magnet and school choice applications open in November and close in January. Attend fall choice information events early — IB and arts magnet programs are competitive and have limited seats.
Charlotte-area families with children in South Carolina suburban schools (Fort Mill, Rock Hill) should verify spring break dates separately — SC districts may break one to two weeks earlier than CMS.

districtFaqs.q: Is Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools the same as UNC Charlotte (UNCC)?
districtFaqs.a: No. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools (CMS) is the K-12 public school system serving Charlotte and Mecklenburg County. The University of North Carolina at Charlotte (UNC Charlotte or UNCC) is a separate public research university with its own semester academic calendar. UNCC's schedule has no connection to CMS school dates.

districtFaqs.q: How do Charlotte-Mecklenburg spring break dates compare to South Carolina suburbs like Fort Mill or Rock Hill?
districtFaqs.a: Charlotte-area suburbs in South Carolina — including Fort Mill, Rock Hill, and Indian Land in York County, SC — are separate school districts in a different state with different academic calendars. South Carolina districts may start earlier than CMS (in early August) and have spring break dates that differ by one to two weeks. Always check the official calendar for each specific school district.

districtFaqs.q: When does CMS magnet school enrollment open?
districtFaqs.a: Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools opens its magnet and school choice application process in November for the following school year. Applications for most programs close in January. Families interested in IB, performing arts, STEM, or language immersion programs should attend a fall choice information fair and apply before the January deadline — popular programs fill quickly.

districtFaqs.q: Why does CMS start school later than Wake County or Cumberland County?
districtFaqs.a: Each North Carolina school district sets its own academic calendar subject to state requirements. CMS starts August 25 — the latest of NC's four largest districts — while Wake County begins as early as August 6 and Cumberland County around August 7. The later start means CMS also ends later (June 9), which is important for families planning summer camps and childcare. The trade-off is a start date closer to what families relocating from northeastern or midwestern states typically expect.

districtFaqs.q: Does CMS serve students in Union County or Cabarrus County?
districtFaqs.a: No. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools serves only Mecklenburg County. Union County Public Schools and Cabarrus County Schools are separate districts with their own academic calendars. Families buying homes in Waxhaw, Marvin, Indian Trail, or other Union County communities — even those that feel like Charlotte suburbs — will be in Union County Public Schools, not CMS. Similarly, Concord and Kannapolis are in Cabarrus County Schools. Verify your specific address before assuming CMS enrollment.

districtFaqs.q: Do NASCAR race weekends at Charlotte Motor Speedway affect school schedules?
districtFaqs.a: NASCAR races at Charlotte Motor Speedway in Concord do not result in CMS school closures or calendar changes. However, major race weekends — including the Coca-Cola 600 in late May (near the end of the school year) and the Bank of America ROVAL in October — bring significant traffic to I-85 north of Charlotte. Families driving to or from schools in northeast Charlotte and Cabarrus County should allow extra time on race-weekend school days and evenings.

livingHere.intro: Charlotte is the largest city in North Carolina and one of the fastest-growing metros in the Southeast, powered by a banking and financial services sector that has drawn major corporate headquarters and tens of thousands of relocating families over the past two decades. The city straddles the NC-SC border, making cross-state commuting and school district coordination a routine consideration for many CMS families.

highlights.label: Charlotte Douglas Airport (CLT)
highlights.detail: Charlotte Douglas International is American Airlines' second-largest hub and one of the ten busiest airports in the US, with nonstop service to over 175 destinations. The week after CMS's June 9 last day is one of the busiest family travel periods at CLT — book summer flights before spring break. Good Friday (March 26) plus spring break week (March 29–April 2) creates a nine-day travel window; airfare and popular beach routes fill quickly once CMS releases its calendar in fall. Families waiting on magnet placement results (typically late February) should book spring break flights immediately after results arrive — waiting until March risks higher fares.

highlights.label: Corporate Relocation into CMS
highlights.detail: Charlotte's banking and finance sector — Bank of America, Wells Fargo, Truist, Honeywell — generates year-round corporate transfers. New hire families often arrive mid-semester, making CMS one of North Carolina's highest mid-year enrollment districts. Families relocating via employer transfer should contact CMS enrollment before the move to start paperwork early.

highlights.label: NASCAR Hall of Fame and Motorsports
highlights.detail: The NASCAR Hall of Fame is in Uptown Charlotte, and over 200 racing teams and suppliers are based in the metro area. Fall race weekends at Charlotte Motor Speedway (Concord) draw large crowds and affect traffic on I-85 north of the city.

highlights.label: Carowinds Theme Park
highlights.detail: Carowinds, straddling the NC-SC state line in south Charlotte, is a perennial CMS family destination with a season that aligns closely to the school calendar. Season passes purchased in winter pay off quickly: Opening Weekend (typically late March) falls just before spring break, giving pass holders their first visits at lighter crowds before the break week rush. Spring Break week (March 29–April 2) and Memorial Day weekend are the two peak days of the season — families with passes who prefer shorter lines should go Opening Weekend or weekday afternoons instead. The park runs through mid-August, giving CMS families a destination right up to the August 25 first day of school.

highlights.label: Lake Norman — End-of-Year and Summer
highlights.detail: Lake Norman, a 32,000-acre reservoir 25 miles north of Charlotte, is the dominant summer destination for CMS families in northern Mecklenburg. Boat launches and lakeside parks get crowded the weekend after CMS's June 9 last day. Summer camps on Lake Norman fill in March — register once magnet placement results arrive in late February.

highlights.label: US National Whitewater Center
highlights.detail: The US National Whitewater Center in west Charlotte is a year-round outdoor hub that CMS families use across the full school calendar. Summer camps there book out quickly after CMS's June 9 last day — register before spring break if you want a spot in July or early August. On teacher workdays and CMS non-school days, the center is a common alternative to childcare; check the district calendar for fall and spring workdays and plan accordingly. Weekend and after-school memberships are popular among CMS families from September through May, making it a practical activity anchor during the school year, not just summer.

highlights.label: South Carolina Border Communities
highlights.detail: Fort Mill, Rock Hill, and Indian Land in York County, SC are effectively Charlotte suburbs but fall under South Carolina school districts with different calendars. Families coordinating between CMS and SC districts should verify spring break dates separately, as they can differ by 1–2 weeks.

sources.label: North Carolina Department of Public Instruction
